home.title: NTHU Observatory
home.subtitle: National Tsing Hua University Observatory
home.aboutHeading: A benchmark in Taiwan's astronomical education
home.aboutP1: The NTHU Observatory was established in 1971 when Professor Qiu Hongyi led a group of students in handcrafting telescopes from scratch. Today it houses the largest refracting telescope in Taiwan — a 25 cm refractor — and continues its mission of education and public outreach.
home.aboutP2: Located on the 8th floor of the Physics Building at National Tsing Hua University in Hsinchu, the observatory offers guided visits, public open days, and real-time weather monitoring for observation planning.
home.exploring: Exploring the cosmos from Hsinchu, Taiwan
home.visitHeading: Bring your group to the observatory
home.visitDesc: We welcome educational institutions and non-profit organizations for guided visits. Groups of 10–25 people, by appointment only. Apply at least one month in advance.

services.title: Guided Visits
services.desc: Group visits for educational institutions. 10–25 people per group, by appointment, one month in advance.

services.title: Public Open Days
services.desc: Occasional open days where the public can explore the observatory and interact with our telescopes.

services.title: Weather Monitoring
services.desc: Real-time sky conditions from our all-sky camera and weather station for observation planning.

services.title: Astronomical Education
services.desc: Supporting astronomy education at NTHU and serving as a benchmark for Taiwan's public outreach.

services.title: Observation Sessions
services.desc: Regular sessions using our 25 cm refractor, 16-inch Cassegrain, and Celestron 8 telescopes.

services.title: News & Gallery
services.desc: Updates on observations, events, and astronomical imagery captured at the observatory.

about.title: About the Observatory
about.p1: The NTHU Observatory was established in 1971 by Professor Qiu Hongyi, who returned from the United States to teach at the Physics Department of National Tsing Hua University. Together with a group of motivated students, he ground an 8-inch Newtonian mirror, built a mirror grinding machine, and later completed a 16-inch Cassegrain reflector — entirely by hand.
about.p2: In November 1971, all instruments atop the old Physics Building were installed and calibrated, and the founding meeting of the Astronomy Club was held. The observatory and the club were inseparable in those early years.
about.p3: In 1988, the old Physics Building was demolished and the department relocated to its current location beside the sports field. The observatory was rebuilt in 1991 through the efforts of Professors Shen Junshan and Zhou Dingyi, returning to Physics Department management and formally separating from the Astronomy Club.
about.p4: After more than two decades of use, the facility fell into disrepair. In 2018, then-department chair Yu Yide initiated a major renovation campaign with alumni from the class of 1978. Under the planning of Professor Lai Shiping of the Institute of Astronomy, the equatorial mount and dome were fully renovated in May 2019.
about.p5: In 2020, the observatory launched its Facebook, Instagram, and YouTube channels. As one of Taiwan's benchmarks for astronomical science education, it houses the largest aperture refractor in Taiwan — a 25 cm (10-inch) refractor — and continues to serve the public through guided visits, open days, and online resources.
about.timelineNote: Source: Interview of 黃銘爵 by 謝立宜, Republic of China Year 69 (1980)

timeline.event: Professor Qiu Hongyi returns from the US
Grinding of the 8-inch Newtonian mirror begins
8-inch Newtonian telescope and grinding machine completed
16-inch fully installed and calibrated; founding meeting held

photos.caption: Former observatory on the old Physics Building.
16-inch handmade Cassegrain reflector with open fork mount.
Two Celestron 8 telescopes imported from the US.

equipment.name: 25 cm Refractor
equipment.detail: The largest aperture refracting telescope in Taiwan. Permanently mounted.

equipment.name: 16-inch Cassegrain Reflector
equipment.detail: Handmade by students in 1971. Open fork equatorial mount.

equipment.name: Celestron 8 (×2)
equipment.detail: Schmidt-Cassegrain telescopes imported from the United States.

equipment.name: All-Sky Camera
equipment.detail: Full-sky imaging for real-time cloud cover and weather monitoring.

equipment.name: Weather Station
equipment.detail: Monitors temperature, humidity, wind speed, pressure, and rainfall.

people.title: People
people.studentDesc: The observatory is operated by a student team from the Institute of Astronomy at NTHU. The team is renewed each academic year and is responsible for all day-to-day operations, guided visits, and public outreach activities.
people.joinDesc: Interested in joining the observatory management team? We welcome NTHU students with a passion for astronomy and public outreach. Reach out via email to learn about current openings.

roles.desc: Oversees daily operations and coordinates observation sessions.
Operates telescopes and equipment during observation sessions.
Leads guided tours and educational visits for groups.

calendar.label: Events & Schedule
calendar.title: Calendar
calendar.hoursNote: Hours vary each semester and are published at the start of term. Summer and winter breaks require separate coordination.
calendar.subscribeDesc: Add our calendar to Google Calendar to stay updated on open days and observation events.

visit.label: Live and learn
visit.title: Visit Registration
visit.policyDesc: The NTHU Observatory accepts group visits for the purpose of astronomical science education and public outreach. A maximum of 2 groups per month (1 group per day), by appointment only. Priority is given to educational institutions. The same organization may visit a maximum of 2 times per year.
visit.eligibilityDesc: Non-profit organizations and schools with a genuine interest in astronomy. Groups of 10–25 people per visit. For groups larger than 25, please contact us or apply in separate sessions. Individual applications are not accepted.
visit.timesDesc: Hours are determined each semester and announced at the start of term. Summer and winter breaks require separate coordination.
visit.durationDesc: Maximum 1 hour. The route and accessible areas are determined by on-site staff. Please maintain a low volume as the observatory is located in a teaching facility.
visit.deadlineDesc: Apply by the 15th of the month prior to your intended visit. For example, to visit in October, apply by September 15th. Submit the registration form linked in the sidebar.
visit.approvalDesc: Applications are reviewed and approved by the NTHU Observatory Management Team (Institute of Astronomy, NTHU). You will be notified of the result.
notes: If you cannot attend after confirmation, notify us at least one week before the scheduled date via nthuobs@gmail.com. Failure to do so may result in the organization being ineligible for future applications.
The observatory reserves the right to reschedule visits in the event of typhoons, natural disasters, or emergencies.
Visitors must follow the directions of observatory staff at all times. Those who do not comply may be asked to leave.
The applicant (group leader) is responsible for the safety of all group members. The observatory is not liable for any incidents during the visit.
For elementary school groups: students must be Grade 3 or above, and at least 2 teachers must accompany the group.
These rules are effective from the date of publication and are subject to revision.
visit.address: Room 801, Physics Building, National Tsing Hua University
visit.addressDetail: No. 101, Sec. 2, Guangfu Rd., East District, Hsinchu City 30013, Taiwan
visit.elevatorNote: (Take the elevator to the top floor, then walk up the remaining stairs.)
visit.hw1Desc: Take the Hsinchu System interchange, exit at Guangfu Rd. Head toward the city, turn left onto Guangfu Rd., and continue 3–5 minutes to the main gate.
visit.hw3Desc: Exit at Zhudong / Qionglin, follow signs toward Hsinchu Science Park, continue approximately 30 minutes to Guangfu Rd., then turn right.
visit.trainDesc: Alight at Hsinchu Station (front exit). Walk between SOGO and Eslite, take Bus No. 1, 2, or 1A (NT$15) — approximately 15–20 minutes to campus.
visit.busDesc: Alight near the Guangfu Rd. exit and walk or take a local bus 5–10 minutes to campus.
visit.questionsDesc: For enquiries not covered by these rules, please email us directly.
visit.rulesPassed: Passed by the NTHU Observatory Management Team
